title: Lesson 321
transcript: Patch management, a proactive process we use to create a consistently configured environment that is secure against known vulnerabilities. Very helpful for me to read the definition there, right? So I mean, what is this actually telling us? One thing, start with the very first word, they're proactive. Patch management is absolutely a proactive process. Organizations have to proactively be making sure, are there new vulnerabilities out there? Are there new patches available? Are there new threats that are applicable to our environment so that we know we need to patch our environment? The next part of it there is to create a consistently configured environment. You want to make sure that if you've got 100 servers that are all the same operating system, the same type, that all of them are properly patched, consistently configured. And the final part there is why do we actually do patching? Well, the whole reason we do patching is kind of two major reasons. One, we want to implement some new functionality, some new capabilities into our system, or to fix some flaw, something broken, some vulnerability. And that's what that last part relates to. You can only patch against known vulnerabilities, which again relates to the fact that you've got to know when you're supposed to patch your system. So here's what the patch management lifecycle looks like. And this is not one of these things that you need to memorize. You don't have to memorize the whole patch management process. And really, I only want to spend time on the first three. The first of which is monitoring for when a new patch or when there's a new vulnerability out there, a new threat that you have to be concerned about. And I really wanna spend a moment on this because it is critically important for organizations to proactively know when they're supposed to patch their systems. And for some systems, that's very easy. For Windows operating systems, for iPhones, for some of the systems we have out there, it's an easy process because the vendor jabs at us, right? Hey, there's a new update available. There's a new update. And it's very easy to know when you're supposed to patch your system. But for the vast majority of systems hardware out there, the vendors don't proactively tell us that new patches are available. We often have to check things like their Twitter feeds, like their RSS feeds, go to their website to see if they've posted something. Or even more proactively, do threat monitoring. Look out there on the deep dark web, know what new threats are emerging and which of those threats might be applicable to our environment. And then make sure that we are able to patch our environments for those new threats or these new vulnerabilities that have surfaced. And so that's the very first step of patch management is making sure you're proactively knowing when you have to patch. And then once you know, then you can go and acquire the patch, get your hands on it. And then basically the whole rest of the process is change management. Once you know that you need to patch a system, that's now just another change that needs to be made and you follow your change management process. Let's talk about how we determine the patch levels of different systems. It's kind of three approaches that you can use. One is you can use agents. An agent is a little piece of software installed on the device, on the server, on the laptop, on the workstation. So agent-based checking means there's this little software agent and through that agent, you can easily query it and it'll check the patch levels of the system. But using an agent-based approach means we have to have little software agents installed on all of our systems. Another approach is agentless. And this is where there is no little software agent installed on the server. Instead, we let, or we use a system that's able to log into and check and query the system remotely. Perfect example of this. We talked about how we can use a vulnerability scanner in credential mode. In other words, we give the vulnerability scanner the ability to log into a system by giving it a set of credentials. And then it can look at the detailed configuration, what patches are installed on the system. That would be an example of agentless. The final approach we can use is what is known as passive. Passive means we're not using agent, we're not using agentless, we're instead monitoring the system using very subtle techniques. An example of this would be where you're monitoring traffic packets that are coming from the system. And what you could do is, this is actually referred to as fingerprinting. If you very, very carefully look at the packets that are being sent out by a system, you'll find that there's some differences between systems. Although there's standards for how packets are supposed to be created and different protocols are supposed to work, there are very subtle differences on how different systems formulate their packets. And so if you look at this very carefully, you can actually passively detect the exact version of a system by the way it's formulating and creating its packets, or even by sending it specially queried, specially crafted packets and looking at the response. But you can determine a system's patch level without actually having to log into the system. Why would you ever want to do passive detection of patch levels? Well, what if you had a vendor where you're not able to log into that vendor system and check? This is a perfect example of where you might want to passively keep an eye on your vendors to see, are they actually patching their systems? And there's even services that you can pay for that will do this for you. They will passively monitor your vendors and tell you, are they keeping up with their patching? Final thing we'll talk about here is how do you actually deploy the patches? Two major methods, manual or automated. Manual means someone is manually logging into the system, entering or installing the patch and checking to make sure it's installed properly. This is obviously a lot slower, more laborious process. So why and where would you want to do this? Well, for high value systems like servers, it's not uncommon that when you patch a system, it causes problems for the system. It breaks something. And so if it's a very high value system, you probably want to do manual patching so you can very carefully check, do regression testing and so forth to check to make sure you haven't broken anything. Automated, on the other hand, means we use software, we use automated systems to deploy the patches automatically. Perfect example of this, Windows Update or our iPhones. You can push patches, the idea behind automated is you can push patches to hundreds, thousands, tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands of systems simultaneously or in very short order. So where would you want to do automated testing? Well, in an example like that where you've got to update, patch a lot of systems very quickly. So in an organization, a large organization that might have tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands of laptops out there, there's no way that the IT department could actually manually patch all those systems. Instead, it's much better to deploy all the patches to all these systems automatically. And yeah, you're probably going to break a few systems doing this, but it's better to get the vast majority of the systems quickly updated, quickly patched, and then those few systems that break, bring them into IT. So that's manual versus automated.
